message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) is a rapidly expanding global pandemic resulting in significant morbidity and mortality. COVID-19 patients may present with acute myocardial infarction (AMI). The aim of this study is to conduct detailed analysis on patients with AMI and COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We included all patients admitted with AMI and actively known or found to be COVID-19 positive by PCR between the 4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    February 2020 and the 11
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    June 2020 in the State of Qatar. Patients were divided into ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) and Non-STE (NSTEMI).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    There were 68 patients (67 men and 1 woman) admitted between the 4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of February 2020 and the 11
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of June 2020 with AMI and COVID-19. The mean age was 49.1±9 years, 46 patients had STEMI and 22 had NSTEMI. 38% had diabetes mellitus, 31% had hypertension, 16% were smokers, 13% had dyslipidemia, and 14.7% had prior cardiovascular disease. Chest pain and dyspnea were the presenting symptoms in 90% and 12% of patients, respectively. Fever (15%) and cough (15%) were the most common COVID-19 symptoms, while the majority had no viral symptoms. Thirty-nine (33 STEMI and 6 NSTEMI) patients underwent coronary angiography, 38 of them had significant coronary disease. In-hospital MACE was low; 1 patient developed stroke and 2 died.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Contrary to previous small reports, in-hospital adverse events were low in this largest cohort of COVID-19 patients presenting with AMI. We hypothesize patient’s demographics and profile including younger age contributed to these findings. Further studies are required to confirm this observation.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key questions</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this subject?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>COVID-19 patients may present with acute myocardial infarction (AMI).</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What might this study add?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Contrary to previous small reports, most COVID-19 patients presenting with AMI have significant obstructive coronary artery disease and favorable in-hospital outcome.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>How might this impact on clinical practice?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>COVID-19 patients presenting with AMI should be treated according to the standard practice.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: The Outcome of COVID-19 Patients with Acute Myocardial Infarction
